title: Teaching
text: I am prepared to teach the quantitative methods sequence at every level — from introductory statistics through regression, causal inference, and Bayesian and computational methods — as well as courses on political parties, elections, democratization, and East and Southeast Asian politics.

I have also tutored graduate students for the statistics core (POLSCI 599, Fall 2025) in Michigan’s Department of Political Science.

title: Tyler Rongxuan Chen
text:
I am a Ph.D. candidate in Political Science and Scientific Computing at the University of Michigan, where I am also completing an M.A. in Statistics. I study the relationship between political parties and the populations beneath them. Parties and party systems stand on alignments with particular population groups, and my research asks what happens to those alignments when the groups move, grow, shrink, and re-cluster across space and time — and what parties do to defend, adapt, or even engineer the populations that sustain them.
My dissertation works the population side of this relationship, tracing how the changing geographic distribution of critical population groups creates scale effects on parties and party systems in the United States, Malaysia, Taiwan, and Japan. A second strand works the party side: how dominant parties hold their alignments together — through state-directed settlement, mobilizational campaigns, and the management of their own elites — and how they fragment when those efforts fail.
Because the groups that matter most are often the ones surveys measure worst, my methodological work develops Bayesian, simulation-based tools — including the Bayesian Approximate Computation with Hierarchical framework (BACH) — that recover group voting behavior from aggregate election data.
My research appears in the Journal of East Asian Studies, and I work with primary sources in Chinese, Malay, and Indonesian. I have taught across Michigan’s quantitative methods sequence, from the graduate statistics core to undergraduate data science, and at the Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research (ICPSR) Summer Program.
I am on the 2026–27 academic job market.

title: Data
text:
When the quantity of interest is unobserved, I build the data. Every paper ships with a journal-grade replication archive; the datasets below are released, or built for release, as standalone public resources.
Malaysia Election Panel Data, 1959–2018. A constituency-level panel covering every Malaysian general election, built from primary electoral records with boundary harmonization across successive redistricting exercises. Available at Zenodo.
Lower Chamber Elections Archive, Malaysia. Contributed to the Constituency-Level Elections Archive (CLEA), including candidate-, district-, and party-coding corrections fed back to the archive.
Three-Country Candidate Career Panels. Career-trajectory panels for every dominant-party candidate in three regimes — the United Malays National Organisation (UMNO) in Malaysia (1959–2022), the Liberal Democratic Party (LDP) in Japan (1947–2014), and the Kuomintang (KMT) in Taiwan (1986–2024) — assembled from primary records in three languages with documented codebooks. Public release planned alongside the associated paper, “Crisis from Within.”
Geocoded Settlement Layers for Peninsular Malaysia. Geocoded layers of Emergency-era Chinese New Villages and Federal Land Development Authority (FELDA) schemes, joined to historical constituency boundaries for every delineation since 1955, with census-interpolated ethnic composition. Released through the replication archives of the associated papers.

title: Research
text:
My research studies the relationship between political parties and the populations beneath them. A party system is an alignment: parties stand not on individual voters but on territorially arranged population groups, and the same electorate can sustain one party coalition under one spatial arrangement and break it under another. My work asks how that alignment is made, strained, and remade — from both sides.
From the population side, urbanization, migration, and generational replacement re-sort the groups that parties stand on. My Journal of East Asian Studies article shows that Malaysia’s celebrated urban–rural divide is largely an artifact of aggregation, leaving ethnicity the dominant axis of competition; a working paper traces how urbanization dismantled the ruling coalition’s patronage machine settlement by settlement, decades before its formal defeat; and a dissertation component in development follows Latino voters in the United States as they move and cluster. From the party side, parties adapt to these changes and sometimes create them: regimes engineered the settlement geographies their machines ran on, dominant parties keep the state subordinate to political ends through mobilizational campaigns, and when the alignment fails from within, elites defect — and their breakaway parties survive only by attaching to population groups the parent cannot absorb.
Because the groups that matter most are often the ones surveys measure worst, the methodological strand of my work builds new tools — above all the Bayesian Approximate Computation with Hierarchical framework (BACH), a simulation-based ecological-inference estimator for subgroup voting behavior under survey nonresponse, partisan polarization, and cross-cutting cleavages.

title: Research
text:
Peer-Reviewed Publications
“Myth and Measurement: Spatial Heterogeneity, Ecological Fallacy, and the Urban Narrative in Peninsular Malaysia’s Electoral Politics.” 2026. Journal of East Asian Studies 26(1): 87–112. [Open Access] [Replication]
Aggregate election returns and surveys point the same way once spatial heterogeneity is modeled: the apparent urban–rural divide is largely an artifact of aggregation, and ethnicity remains the dominant cleavage. Aggregate election data, modeled carefully, can answer questions where good surveys are scarce.
“Social Transformation in Malaysia and the Fragmentation of the Malay-Dominant Political Parties — Based on a Social Cleavage Structure Theory Perspective” (with Congcong Fu). Southeast Asian Studies (东南亚研究) 2021(3): 1–29. [Chinese Full Text] [English Translation]
“Social Transformation, Dispute over Routes and the Split of Malaysian Islamic Party (PAS)” (with Congcong Fu). Southeast Asian Affairs (南洋问题研究) 2020(1): 79–95. [Chinese Full Text] [English Translation]

title: Research
text:
Under Review
“Morning News: Public Signals, Elite Defections, and the Collective Abdication of Authoritarian Regimes.”
When a survival crisis breaks, dispersed regime elites coordinate their exit through public signals — the morning’s front page. A global game joined to an original day-by-day elite-stance dataset from Indonesia’s 1998 transition shows that leaders fall where wide public channels meet leader-specific demands.
“Colonial Institutionalization and Unborn Civil Society in Post-Colonial States: Malaysia and Indonesia in Comparative Perspective.”
Why do some communal institutions survive decades of hostile policy while comparable ones next door are extinguished? Colonial decisions to register, codify, or spatially contain civil-sphere domains set the institutional terrain on which communal politics in Malaysia and Indonesia still runs.
“Symbolic Power and the Functional Divide: Campaigns Across the Party-State Spectrum.”
Across the party-state spectrum, recurrent mobilizational campaigns are the instrument by which a party keeps the state it built subordinate to political ends — evidence from seven decades of the Chinese party press, firm-level event studies, and daily social-media discourse.

title: Research
text:
Working Papers
“What Race and Ethnicity Hide: Bayesian Approximate Computation with Hierarchical Framework (BACH) for Subgroup Voting Behavior under Partisan Polarization, Cross-Cutting Cleavages, and Survey Nonresponse.” [SSRN]
A simulation-based ecological-inference framework that recovers subgroup voting behavior from aggregate election returns under the three conditions that defeat existing tools simultaneously — validated in large-scale simulation and against statewide voter-file ground truth. Substantively, national origin rather than education organizes Hispanic vote choice, a within-race cleavage that race-level estimators collapse.
“Research Note: A Higher-Order Method of Moments Approach to Ecological Inference under Extreme Survey Nonresponse.” [SSRN]
Higher-order moments of the cross-unit distribution of aggregate outcomes identify group voting behavior when surveys are unreliable or absent — aimed at historical elections and democracies without polling infrastructure.
“Bridges Burnt: Uneven Urbanization and the Spatial Breakdown of Patronage Infrastructure in Malaysia.” [Latest Draft]
Patronage is a delivery technology that depends on geographic social closure. Urbanization dissolved that closure settlement type by settlement type — Chinese New Villages near towns before Malay land schemes in the interior — dismantling Malaysia’s ruling-coalition machine decades before its 2018 defeat.
“Crisis from Within: Elite Defection and Intraparty Competition in Dominant-Party Regimes.”
Elites defect from dominant parties at the height of party strength: losing an internal contest forces a choice between marginalization and exit, and breakaway parties endure only when they attach to a social cleavage the parent cannot absorb. Built on candidate-level career panels covering every United Malays National Organisation (UMNO), Liberal Democratic Party (LDP), and Kuomintang (KMT) candidate across each party’s dominant era.
“Inducing Measurement Error? Fixed Effect under Asymptotic Conditions” (with Robert Franzese).
Asks when fixed-effects specifications induce, rather than remove, bias under asymptotic conditions.
“Plant Closures and Trump-Related Twitter Discussion: A Spatial-Temporal Description” (with Robert Franzese). [Latest Draft]
Traces how local plant-closing shocks shaped Trump-related and extremist engagement on social media across space and time, using a national geocoded tweet corpus.
